EDCS-10400970.translation: To Maxsumus, son of Ateula, and Maius, son of Maxsumus, and Ica, daughter of Congonnetiacus. Fabatus, son of Maxsumus, and Comnitsia, his mother, and Secundus, son of Maxsumus, his brother, and Metellus, his freedman, made the wall for me from his own resources.
EDCS-10400970.summary: This funerary inscription records the construction of a burial enclosure (maceria) by freedmen and family members for their patrons or relatives, including Maxsumus, Maius, Ica, and Secundus.

EDCS-10400971.translation: To the shades of the underworld and for the memory of Melausus, deceased, aged 35 years. Marcus S. D. had this set up.
EDCS-10400971.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Melausus, deceased at age 35, likely set up by Marcus S. D.

EDCS-10400972.translation: ... deceased, aged 41 years. Micurita, his wife, had this set up.
EDCS-10400972.summary: This fragment records the death of an individual aged 41 and indicates that his wife, Micurita, arranged for the monument.

EDCS-10400974.translation: Nammia Sulla, daughter of Livius Lugaunus, [daughter] of Aulus Livius Vindicianus. Livius Luganus placed [this].
EDCS-10400974.summary: This inscription records the placing of a monument by Livius Luganus for Nammia Sulla, indicating family relationships within the Roman elite.

EDCS-10400975.translation: [To the shades of the underworld] and for the memory of Nantius, deceased, aged 41 years. His wife and brother had this set up.
EDCS-10400975.summary: This funerary dedication for Nantius, deceased at age 41, was arranged by his wife and brother.

EDCS-10400977.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Titus Nobilius Favor, deceased, aged 41, and of his mother Atreba, deceased, aged 60.
EDCS-10400977.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Titus Nobilius Favor and his mother Atreba.

EDCS-10400979.translation: A friend, deceased, aged 30, saw to the setting up of this monument.
EDCS-10400979.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where a friend commissioned the monument for the deceased, who was 30 years old.

EDCS-10400982.translation: Julius Lentinus, husband, set this up to Pompeia Marituma, to the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-10400982.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Julius Lentinus set up the monument for his wife, Pompeia Marituma.

EDCS-10400983.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Popatea, deceased, aged [..].
EDCS-10400983.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Popatea, who died at an unspecified age.

EDCS-10400984.translation: Popillius Secundinus, son, saw to the setting up of this monument for the deceased, aged 80.
EDCS-10400984.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Popillius Secundinus commissioned a monument for a man who died at the age of 80.

EDCS-10400985.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Gaius Pr[..] Apollonius.
EDCS-10400985.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Gaius Pr[..] Apollonius.

EDCS-10400986.translation: To the eternal memory of Procla, deceased, aged 17.
EDCS-10400986.summary: This funerary epitaph records the eternal memory of Procla, who died at the age of 17.

EDCS-10400987.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Publicius Matonis, freedman, deceased, aged 65. Minister, his son-in-law, set this up.
EDCS-10400987.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication to Publicius Matonis, a freedman aged 65, by his son-in-law Minister.

EDCS-10400989.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment dedicated to Publicia.

EDCS-10400990.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Publicia Adiusta, deceased, aged 57.
EDCS-10400990.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication to Publicia Adiusta, who died at the age of 57.

EDCS-10400991.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Publicia Carasova, who died at the age of 27. (This monument) was erected by [her] caretaker.
EDCS-10400991.summary: A funerary dedication for Publicia Carasova, who died at 27, erected by her caretaker.

EDCS-10400992.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of (a man) who died, Pupius Getaus, under the age of 45.
EDCS-10400992.summary: A funerary dedication for Pupius Getaus, who died at an unspecified age under 45.

EDCS-10400993.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Romulus and Victoriae; (his) mother erected (this) at 21 years of age.
EDCS-10400993.summary: A funerary dedication for Romulus and Victoriae, with the mother erecting it for a 21-year-old.

EDCS-10400994.translation: Rufina, daughter of Tauricius. Tauricus and Sulpicius Senilis, the heirs, took care to set this up according to the will.
EDCS-10400994.summary: A funerary monument set up by heirs according to a will for Rufina, daughter of Tauricius.

EDCS-10400996.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Sa[...]
EDCS-10400996.summary: An incomplete funerary dedication to a person named Sa[...].

EDCS-10400997.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Sabina, who died at the age of 27.
EDCS-10400997.summary: A funerary dedication for Sabina, who died at 27.

EDCS-10400998.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription possibly indicating a mother erected a monument for Sabina.

EDCS-10401000.summary: A dedication or ownership marker for Sacrapo, son of Coxtus (or a similar name).

EDCS-10401001.translation: To the departed spirits and in memory of Sacrina, who died at the age of [...].
EDCS-10401001.summary: A funerary dedication for Sacrina, with the age of death incomplete.

EDCS-10401002.translation: To the departed spirits. His brother erected (this) for Saio Tertius Veciso.
EDCS-10401002.summary: A funerary dedication by a brother for Saio Tertius Veciso.

EDCS-10401004.translation: To the shades and memory of Lucius Samonius Caedianus, most loving husband to Julia Castrensis, and to Julia Victoris, [his] husband.
EDCS-10401004.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Julia Castrensis and Julia Victoris, likely by Lucius Samonius Caedianus.

EDCS-10401005.translation: Samoni, deceased, aged 15 years. Julius Gracil<is> <his> owner erected [this].
EDCS-10401005.summary: A funerary epitaph for Samoni, erected by his owner, Julius Gracil<is>.

EDCS-10401006.translation: To the shades and memory of Sanctia Verecunda, deceased, aged 30 years. Severianus, [her] son, saw to its placement.
EDCS-10401006.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sanctia Verecunda, erected by her son Severianus.

EDCS-10401007.translation: To the shades. Saturnina, <his> companion, erected [this] for Politimus.
EDCS-10401007.summary: A funerary epitaph for Politimus, erected by his companion Saturnina.

EDCS-10401008.translation: To the memory of Saturninia Atturita(?), deceased, aged 41 years. Gaius Cat(onius?) Seno [erected this] for [his] wife.
EDCS-10401008.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturninia Atturita(?), erected by her husband Gaius Catonius Seno.

EDCS-10401009.translation: [---] Saturni, aged 45. Saturn<in>us erected [this] of <his> own [expense?].
EDCS-10401009.summary: A funerary epitaph for Saturni, aged 45, with Saturninus responsible for its erection.

EDCS-10401010.translation: To the shades and memory of Secunda, deceased, aged 22 years.
EDCS-10401010.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secunda, deceased at 22 years of age.

EDCS-10401011.translation: Secundina, deceased, aged 38 years. <Her> patron saw to its placement. V[iator?].
EDCS-10401011.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secundina, erected by her patron.

EDCS-10401012.translation: For Secundinus, deceased, aged 2 years and 7 months. Macrinus saw to [this].
EDCS-10401012.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secundinus, aged 2 years and 7 months, placed by Macrinus.

EDCS-10401013.translation: To the shades. Secundinus, deceased, aged 12 years. Secundina, <his> mother, saw to its placement.
EDCS-10401013.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secundinus, aged 12, erected by his mother Secundina.

EDCS-10401014.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely naming Caius Secundus Bloxius.

EDCS-10401015.translation: To the divine spirits. Secundia Urbana placed [this] for Lucius Secundius Cintucnatus and his wife Claudia Matua and their daughter Senodonna.
EDCS-10401015.summary: This funerary inscription records Secundia Urbana dedicating a monument to her family members Lucius Secundius Cintucnatus, Claudia Matua, and Senodonna.

EDCS-10401017.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription beginning with 'To the divine spirits' and the name Sedatus.

EDCS-10401019.translation: To the divine spirits. Sen(i)ulus(?), son of Cumpirtus(?), died at 17 years of age. His father placed [this].
EDCS-10401019.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Sen(i)ulus, son of Cumpirtus, at the age of 17, with his father as the dedicator.

EDCS-10401020.translation: To the divine spirits. Severilla placed [this] for the memory of her freedman servant Seno, who died at 28 years of age.
EDCS-10401020.summary: This funerary inscription records Severilla dedicating a monument to her deceased freedman servant Seno, who was 28 years old.

EDCS-10401021.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely naming Caius Serenus.

EDCS-10401022.translation: To the divine spirits. Severa, daughter of Severus, died at 19 years of age. Severus, her father, [placed this] for Iardarius.
EDCS-10401022.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Severa, daughter of Severus, at 19, with her father Severus dedicating it.

EDCS-10401023.translation: To the divine spirits. In memory of Titus Severus Primitivus, who died at 30 years of age. Titus Severius Secundus, his patron, arranged for this to be placed.
EDCS-10401023.summary: This funerary inscription records Titus Severius Secundus, the patron, arranging for a monument for his deceased client Titus Severus Primitivus, who died at 30.

EDCS-10401025.translation: To the divine spirits. Aulus Silvius Divixto
EDCS-10401025.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Aulus Silvius Divixto.

EDCS-10401027.translation: To the divine spirits. Matuirus, brother, for his freedwoman Siora Censorina.
EDCS-10401027.summary: This funerary inscription records Matuirus dedicating a monument to his freedwoman and perhaps sister, Siora Censorina.

EDCS-10401028.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Neptacus, father and grandfather, had this erected for his son and grandson, Solinus Neptacus and Sextinus Aulicus.
EDCS-10401028.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Neptacus for his son and grandson, Solinus Neptacus and Sextinus Aulicus.

EDCS-10401029.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sulpicia Severa, wife, had this erected at her own expense for Marcus Sulpicius Criton, deceased, aged 35.
EDCS-10401029.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by Sulpicia Severa for her husband, Marcus Sulpicius Criton, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-10401030.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Julius Magnus (?) had this erected for Sulpicius Miseno, deceased, aged [3?] years.
EDCS-10401030.summary: This is a funerary epitaph, likely erected by Julius Magnus (?), for Sulpicius Miseno, who died aged around 3 years.

EDCS-10401031.translation: To Gaius Sulpicius N[---], aged 45. Julia Taurina, wife, had this erected.
EDCS-10401031.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by Julia Taurina for her husband, Gaius Sulpicius N[---], who died at the age of 45.

EDCS-10401032.translation: To Aulus Sulpicius Paterculus VN[---]. The heirs of Paterculus.
EDCS-10401032.summary: This inscription records the heirs of Aulus Sulpicius Paterculus, indicating a family legacy or property.

EDCS-10401033.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Sulpicia Matua, deceased, aged 70. Her son-in-law had this erected.
EDCS-10401033.summary: This funerary epitaph was set up by the son-in-law for Sulpicia Matua, commemorating her life and death at age 70.

EDCS-10401034.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Sulpicia Rosicia, deceased.
EDCS-10401034.summary: This incomplete funerary epitaph is dedicated to the memory of Sulpicia Rosicia.

EDCS-10401035.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Sumilia Centurionis, daughter, deceased, aged 30. Aurelia, daughter and heir, had this erected.
EDCS-10401035.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Sumilia Centurionis at age 30 and was erected by her daughter and heir, Aurelia.

EDCS-10401037.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records the death of a woman aged 77 and mentions the erection of a sarcophagus.

EDCS-10401041.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the memory of Trilicius, deceased, aged 35. Pixticenus had this erected.
EDCS-10401041.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by Pixticenus for Trilicius, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-10401045.translation: To Valeria Hiberina. Her mother Quartilla set this up, and also to her most devoted husband Publius Lucius. She set this up.
EDCS-10401045.summary: A funerary monument set up by a mother for her daughter Valeria Hiberina and her husband.

EDCS-10401047.translation: To the divine spirits, for the memory of Veneria, deceased, aged 25.
EDCS-10401047.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of the deceased Veneria, aged 25.

EDCS-10401048.translation: To the divine spirits. Her children Verecunda donated (this).
EDCS-10401048.summary: A funerary inscription stating that Verecunda's children donated the monument.

EDCS-10401049.translation: To the divine spirits. Vervicia, deceased, aged 30, provided for this; her mother donated and set this up.
EDCS-10401049.summary: A funerary inscription indicating that Vervicia's mother provided for and donated the monument for her deceased daughter, aged 30.

EDCS-10401050.translation: Of Viator, deceased, aged 61. His wife set this up. A citizen of Vienna?
EDCS-10401050.summary: A funerary epitaph for Viator, who died at 61, set up by his wife, possibly a citizen of Vienna.

EDCS-10401051.translation: To the divine spirits. To Victa.
EDCS-10401051.summary: A brief funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased Victa.

EDCS-10401052.translation: To the divine spirits. Vindicianus, deceased, aged 70.
EDCS-10401052.summary: A funerary inscription for Vindicianus, who died at 70.

EDCS-10401053.translation: To the divine spirits and for the memory of Vipsania Censoria.
EDCS-10401053.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the memory of Vipsania Censoria.

EDCS-10401054.translation: To the divine spirits. Volucer, brother of Constantius, set this up.
EDCS-10401054.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Volucer for his brother Constantius.

EDCS-10401055.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulattia Venantia, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-10401056.translation: To the shades of the departed, her husband placed this for Ursa, deceased at 33 years of age.
EDCS-10401056.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Ursa, erected by her husband.

EDCS-10401057.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph, likely for a woman named S(e)lumia.

EDCS-10401061.translation: [...] an XXXI, eighty-one years old. [...] placed.
EDCS-10401061.summary: A fragmented epitaph possibly recording the age of death for an individual.

EDCS-10401066.translation: [...]ae Etta[...] placed this for herself while alive.
EDCS-10401066.summary: A funerary epitaph for Etta, who dedicated it for herself during her lifetime.

EDCS-10401113.translation: And to the god Apollo Cobledulitavus, Marcus Pompeius Libo, son of Gaius Pompeius Sanctus, a priest of the Arenses, the priest of the Arenses, restored at his own expense the temple of the goddess Tutela and the public baths, both of which had formerly fallen into ruin through age. He paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10401113.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a temple to the goddess Tutela and public baths by Marcus Pompeius Libo, a local priest, likely in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-10401134.text_edition: \n COR Aulus Pomp Dumnomotus tribunus militum legionis IV Scythicae praefectus fabrum amphitheatrum cum ornamentis omnibus de sua pecunia dedit Aulus Pomp Auli Pomp Tertulli filius s perficiendum curavit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-10401134.translation: Aulus Pomp Dumnomotus, son of Aulus Pomp Tertullus, tribune of the soldiers of the IV Scythica legion and prefect of the engineers, dedicated the amphitheater with all its furnishings at his own expense, and saw to its completion and dedicated it himself.
EDCS-10401134.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of an amphitheater by Aulus Pomp Dumnomotus, a military officer, likely in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-10401138.translation: Lucius Marullius Aeternus, son of Lucius Marullius Arabus, of the tribe Quirina, twice chief magistrate, gave the water supply and its conduits at his own expense.
EDCS-10401138.summary: An inscription recording the provision of water supply and its infrastructure by Lucius Marullius Aeternus, a local magistrate, likely in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-10401141.translation: Publius Vinicius Secundus, son of Publius, of the tribe Papiria, aedile, twice chief magistrate, quaestor of the colony Colonia Iulia Paterna Claudia Narbonensis Martius, [dedicated] at his own expense.
EDCS-10401141.summary: An inscription listing the offices held by Publius Vinicius Secundus and stating he made a dedication at his own expense in Narbo Martius, likely in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-10401166.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary epitaph for Iulia Adruppa.

EDCS-10401202.translation: With good memory, for Victoria, most distinguished lady, January 22nd.
EDCS-10401202.summary: A dedication or memorial, possibly funerary, for Victoria, dated by month and day.

EDCS-10401218.translation: To Drusus Germanicus Caesar, Gaius Julius Congonnetodubnus, son of (tribe) Voltinia, Victor, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10401218.summary: This is a dedication to Drusus Caesar, likely made by Gaius Julius Victor, a Roman citizen from the Voltinia tribe.

EDCS-10401220.translation: To Gaius Julius Agedilus, son of Fabius, Macro from the territory of the Santones, a duplicarius of the Ala Atectorigiana Gallorum, having completed 32 years of service, a recalled veteran of the Divi Augusti, honoured with a DC (coinage) for the Caesares, DC for the Raeti, with a shield from the fortress of Ircavium, and with golden torcs and armlets, awarded by his fellow soldiers, his heirs Julia Matrona, daughter (of C. Iulius Primulus) and Gaius Julius Primulus, freedman, had this made in accordance with his will.
EDCS-10401220.summary: This epitaph honors Gaius Julius Macro, a veteran soldier from the Santones who served for 32 years and received military decorations, with his heirs ensuring his burial monument was erected.

EDCS-10401307.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae aeternae vi veterani legionis VIII missi honesta missione qui natus tempora <i=F>unxit sua Caius sibi et fratri supra scribto sic sepulchrum <i=E>mp<e=I>nse vivus faciundum curavit et sub ascia dedicavit \n
EDCS-10401307.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for eternal memory: Caius, a veteran of the Eighth Legion, discharged with an honorable discharge, who reached the end of his life, had this tomb made for himself and his brother inscribed above, and dedicated it while alive under the axe.
EDCS-10401307.summary: This is a self-funded tomb inscription by a veteran of the Eighth Legion, dedicated to himself and his brother.

EDCS-10401311.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et Memoriae Otociliae Severae Lugudunensis Martius Augusti libertus coniugi karissimae posuit\n
EDCS-10401311.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for Memory: Martius, freedman of Augustus, set this up for his dearest wife, Otocilia Severa of Lugudunum.
EDCS-10401311.summary: This epitaph records a monument erected by Martius, a freedman of Augustus, for his wife Otocilia Severa from Lugudunum.

EDCS-10500001.text_edition: \n Augusto et Mercurio votum solverunt libentes merito Tiberius Claudius Potitus Titus Flavius Pinus\n
EDCS-10500001.translation: To Augustus and Mercury, Tiberius Claudius Potitus and Titus Flavius Pinus willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-10500001.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tiberius Claudius Potitus and Titus Flavius Pinus to the gods Augustus and Mercury.

EDCS-10500066.translation: To Caesar Germanicus, son of Augustus, Father of the Country, and to Etnosus: Anavus, freedman of Atticus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10500066.summary: This is a votive inscription by Anavus, freedman of Atticus, to Emperor Caesar Germanicus and to a deity named Etnosus.

EDCS-10500203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Monimae Cesti filiae et Servillae uxsori sic suae et filiae et alterae filliae sic quae vocatur NA IMPR\n
EDCS-10500203.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Monima, daughter of Cestus, and for Servilla, his wife, and for her daughter, and for another daughter who is called NA IMPR.
EDCS-10500203.summary: This epitaph commemorates Monima, her mother Servilla, and two daughters, one of whom is named NA IMPR.

EDCS-10500296.text_edition: \n Artis <grammatices> doctor morumque magister Blaesianus Biturix Musarum semper amator hic iacet aeterno devinctus membra sopore\n
EDCS-10500296.translation: Blaesianus Biturix, teacher of grammar and master of morals, always a lover of the Muses, lies here, bound in eternal sleep.
EDCS-10500296.summary: This epitaph commemorates Blaesianus Biturix, a teacher of grammar and morals and a lover of the Muses.

EDCS-10500361.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Aemili Combrici\n
EDCS-10500361.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for the memory of Aemilia Combrica.
EDCS-10500361.summary: This is an epitaph dedicated to the memory of Aemilia Combrica.

EDCS-10500481.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Domitia Lurandia [dedicated this] for Albinius Senator.
EDCS-10500481.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Domitia Lurandia for Albinius Senator.

EDCS-10500489.translation: To Mars Tritullus, the farmer, they willingly and deservedly paid their vow.
EDCS-10500489.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Mars Tritullus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10500490.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, with parts of names remaining.

EDCS-10500491.translation: To the departed spirits and to the memory of Gemina, his most sweet wife. Anticus, her husband, [dedicated this].
EDCS-10500491.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a husband to his deceased wife, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10500494.translation: To the departed spirits. Hermeros [dedicated this].
EDCS-10500494.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits.

EDCS-10500495.translation: To the honor and memory of Lucius Pomponius Bassulus and Lucius Pomponius Balbinus, his most dutiful sons. Lucius Julius Bassianus, their father, and Pomponia Regola, their mother, built and dedicated this temple from its foundation to its completion, along with the surrounding buildings.
EDCS-10500495.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple and associated buildings by parents in honor of their deceased sons, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10500497.translation: To the departed spirits. Camerianus dedicated this to Petronia, his wife, at his own expense.
EDCS-10500497.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a husband to his deceased wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10500498.translation: To the departed spirits. [---] Geminius M[---]...
EDCS-10500498.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, with remnants of a name.

EDCS-10500499.translation: Lucius Severius Severus, son of Lucius Severius, who held all offices in the city and who established this villa from the ground up, and Decimus Severius, the elder of his sons, established [this] for the health of themselves and their family.
EDCS-10500499.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a villa and the provision for the health of the family by Lucius Severius Severus and his son, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-10500500.translation: [---] daughter of Pomp[---] of Valicus, a friend [dedicated this].
EDCS-10500500.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning a daughter of someone named Pomp[---] and a friend, likely funerary or dedicatory.

EDCS-10500502.translation: [---] M[---] Materna, prompted by her will and divine inspiration, gave this statue of Father Liber to Sergius Secundinus, her son, deservedly.
EDCS-10500502.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription of a statue of Liber Pater by a mother to her son, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10500520.translation: To Etruscilla, wife of our Emperor Augustus, from the free citizenry of the Vellavii.
EDCS-10500520.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the Vellavii to Etruscilla, wife of the emperor, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-11801043.summary: This is a personal name inscription, likely a tombstone or a dedication, with no clear date or context.

EDCS-12000435.translation: To Belgonus, the god, Julius Certus willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-12000435.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Belgonus, dedicated by Julius Certus, likely dating to the Roman period when local deities were worshipped.

EDCS-12000436.text_edition: \n Tutelae pro salute Iustini Iulius Paris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12000436.translation: For the safety of Iustinus, Julius Paris willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-12000436.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Julius Paris to the goddess Tutela for the health of Iustinus, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-12000437.translation: Vitalis, son of Bonxus(?), willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-12000437.summary: This is a votive inscription by Vitalis, possibly the son of Bonxus, who fulfilled a vow, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-12000441.translation: Laureia, daughter of Gesellus, willingly paid her vow as deserved.
EDCS-12000441.summary: This is a votive inscription by Laureia, daughter of Gesellus, who fulfilled a vow, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-12000442.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. To Antistia Severiana, dearest wife. Caius Antistius Eutychianus, husband(?).
EDCS-12000442.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Antistia Severiana by her husband, Caius Antistius Eutychianus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12000443.translation: On the death of Gaius Antistius Protogenes, and of the living Antistia Erotene, wife, and of the deceased Antistia Byblis, daughter.
EDCS-12000443.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of Gaius Antistius Protogenes, his wife Antistia Erotene, and their daughter Antistia Byblis, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12000453.text_edition: \n Tutelae Adehio et Capito Adei Harbelestegis structores votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-12000453.translation: To Tutela, Adehius and Capito, sons of Adehius Harbelestegis, willingly paid their vow as deserved.
EDCS-12000453.summary: This is a votive inscription by Adehius and Capito, sons of Adehius Harbelestegis, to the goddess Tutela, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-12000455.translation: To the spirits of the underworld and in memory of Polychronus, deceased, aged 40. Thorybius erected this.
EDCS-12000455.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Polychronus, who died at the age of 40, set up by Thorybius, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-12000464.text_edition: \n Numinibus Augustorum et Magnae Matri Deum Augustae Lucius Pomponius Sexti Pomponi Paterni sacerdotis Arensis filius Quirina Paternus aram taurobolicam posuit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-12000464.translation: To the divine powers of the Emperors and to the Augusta Magna Mater Deum (Great Mother of the Gods), Lucius Pomponius Paternus, son of Sextus Pomponius Paternus, priest of the Arenses, belonging to the tribe Quirina, set up and dedicated an altar for a taurobolium (bull sacrifice).
EDCS-12000464.summary: A private individual dedicated a taurobolic altar to imperial deities and the Great Mother of the Gods, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-12000585.text_edition: \n Numini Augusti deae Souconae Divixtus Silani filius\n
EDCS-12000585.translation: To the divine power of the Emperor and the goddess Soucona, Divixtus, son of Silanus.
EDCS-12000585.summary: A dedication by an individual named Divixtus to the numen of the Emperor and the local goddess Soucona, probably from Roman Gaul.

EDCS-12400047.translation: For Emperor Caesar Lucius [Domitius] Aurelianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul for the third time, father of the country; by the civitas of the Vellavorum, 13 miles.
EDCS-12400047.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road, 13 miles long, by the civitas of the Vellavorum, dedicated to Emperor Aurelian (reigned 270-275 CE).

EDCS-12400053.translation: To our Lords Emperors Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus and Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, by the civitas of the Vellavorum, 3 miles.
EDCS-12400053.summary: The civitas of the Vellavorum records the construction of a road, 3 miles long, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400063.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Cassianius Latinus Postumus, the Invincible, Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the fourth time; by the civitas of the Gabali.
EDCS-12400063.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the civitas of the Gabali to the usurper Emperor Postumus (reigned 260-269 CE).

EDCS-12400065.translation: For Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, twice consul, proconsul, father of the country, and for Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus, Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, twice consul, proconsul, father of the country.
EDCS-12400065.summary: This text is an dedication by the local community to co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and his son Volusianus, who ruled together from 251-253 CE.

EDCS-12400070.translation: For our Lord Emperor Marcus Philippus Augustus and Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, by the civitas of the Arverni, 21 leagues.
EDCS-12400070.summary: The civitas of the Arverni records a distance of 21 leagues, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-12400073.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country; by the Augustonemetum community of the Arverni, 17 leagues.
EDCS-12400073.summary: This inscription records a distance of 17 leagues by the Arverni community at Augustonemetum, dedicated to Emperor Hadrian (reigned 117-138 CE).

EDCS-12400074.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, imperator for the 11th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, designated for the 4th time; by Augustonemetum, 21 miles.
EDCS-12400074.summary: This inscription records a road of 21 miles by the Augustonemetum community, dedicated to Emperor Claudius (reigned 41-54 CE).

EDCS-12400075.translation: For Emperor Caesar Gaius [Messius] Trajanus Quintus [De]cius Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician [power], [---]
EDCS-12400075.summary: A dedication to Emperor Decius (reigned 249-251 CE) that is incomplete.

EDCS-12400079.translation: Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 5th time, emperor 11 times, father of his country, consul for the 3rd time and designated for the 4th, [dedicates this] 21 miles from Augustonemetum.
EDCS-12400079.summary: This is a milestone inscription from 21 miles outside Augustonemetum, dating to the reign of Emperor Claudius.

EDCS-12400080.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest.
EDCS-12400080.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that names Emperor Hadrian, likely dedicating a public work during his reign.

EDCS-12400081.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Maximus Germanicus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, by the Arverni for 36 leagues.
EDCS-12400081.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Aurelian and records a tribute or grant of roads by the Arverni people.

EDCS-12400083.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Cassianio Latinio Postumo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli III patri patriae civitate Arvernorum leugae XXXV\n
EDCS-12400083.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Cassianius Latinus Postumus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, by the Arverni for 35 leagues.
EDCS-12400083.summary: This inscription honors the usurper emperor Postumus and records a tribute or grant of roads by the Arverni people.

EDCS-12400084.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul, by the Lemovices for 34 leagues.
EDCS-12400084.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Gordian III and records a tribute or grant of roads by the Lemovices people.

EDCS-12400086.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul, by the Lemovices for 30 leagues.
EDCS-12400086.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Gordian III and records a tribute or grant of roads by the Lemovices people.

EDCS-12400096.translation: To Emperor Caesar [Gaius] Pius Esuvius Tetricus Pius Felix Augustus, by the civitas Lemovices for 10 [leagues?], by the civitas Lemovices for 35 leagues.
EDCS-12400096.summary: This inscription honors the usurper emperor Tetricus I and possibly records road lengths associated with the Lemovices people.

EDCS-12400100.text_edition: \n Domino orbis et pacis Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli civitas Petrucoriorum leugam\n
EDCS-12400100.translation: To the lord of the world and of peace, Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of his country, proconsul, by the civitas Petrucoriorum for [number illegible] league.
EDCS-12400100.summary: This inscription honors the usurper emperor Florianus and records a contribution or road length by the Petrucorii people.

EDCS-12400101.translation: To our lords, Emperors Caesars Gaius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Maximian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and to Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, most noble Caesars, by the civitas Nitiobrogum for 6 leagues.
EDCS-12400101.summary: This inscription honors the Tetrarchs (Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius) and records a tribute or road length by the Nitobroges people.

EDCS-12400102.translation: To our lord Emperor Flavius Valerius Constantius, Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power.
EDCS-12400102.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantius I, likely as part of a public work or milestone, and records his titles.

EDCS-12400103.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Imperatore Caesare Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio nobilissimo Caesare divi Constanti Pii Augusti\n
EDCS-12400103.translation: To our Lord the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantine, most noble and pious Caesar, son of the deified Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-12400103.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, likely for a public work or building, dated to his reign (306-337 CE).

EDCS-12400111.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, pious, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Magnus, grandson of the deified Septimius Severus. 11 leagues from Limonum and 10 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400111.summary: This inscription records a milestone or boundary marker relating to Emperor Severus Alexander, dating it to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-12400163.translation: To the Emperor Nerva Trajan, son of the deified Nerva, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul for the 27th time.
EDCS-12400163.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Trajan, likely for a public work, noting his extensive titles and consulship, suggesting a date around 103 CE.

EDCS-12400170.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Pius Esuvius Tetricus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul. 16 leagues from Civitas Pictorum and 20 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400170.summary: This inscription records a milestone or boundary marker for the usurper Tetricus I, likely related to the province of Aquitania, dated to his reign (271-274 CE).

EDCS-12400171.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus, pontifex maximus, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power, consul for the second time. 16 leagues from Civitas Pictorum and 20 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400171.summary: This inscription records a milestone or boundary marker for Emperor Tacitus, likely related to the province of Aquitania, dated to his short reign (275-276 CE).

EDCS-12400175.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. 9 leagues from Limonum and 7 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400175.summary: This is a milestone or boundary marker for Emperor Antoninus Pius, specifying distances from Limonum and borders, dated to his reign (138-161 CE), likely around 140-142 CE.

EDCS-12400176.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. 10 leagues from Limonum and 6 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400176.summary: This is a milestone or boundary marker for Emperor Antoninus Pius, specifying distances from Limonum and borders, dated to his reign (138-161 CE), likely around 140-142 CE.

EDCS-12400177.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time. 10 leagues from Limonum and 6 leagues from the borders.
EDCS-12400177.summary: This is a milestone or boundary marker for Emperor Hadrian, specifying distances from Limonum and borders, dated to his reign (117-138 CE), likely around 124 CE.

EDCS-12400227.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus. 13 leagues from Avaricum.
EDCS-12400227.summary: This inscription records a milestone or boundary marker for Emperor Tacitus, indicating a distance from Avaricum, dated to his short reign (275-276 CE).

EDCS-13302992.text_edition: \n Plotinae Imperatoris Traiani Caesaris Augusti Germanici Caius Iulius Serenus in nomine suo et Iuliae Luci filiae Iuliolae uxoris\n
EDCS-13302992.translation: Gaius Iulius Serenus, in his own name and that of his daughter Iuliola, daughter of Lucius, and his wife Iulia, to Plotina, wife of the Emperor Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus.
EDCS-13302992.summary: This is a dedication from Gaius Iulius Serenus and his family to Empress Plotina, wife of Emperor Trajan, likely for a private religious or public benefaction, dated to Trajan's reign (98-117 CE).

EDCS-13303047.text_edition: \n Caius Antistius Antistiae Rufinae libertus Threptus IIIIIIvir Augustalis sedes de sua pecunia dedit\n
EDCS-13303047.translation: The freedman Threptus, one of the six imperial priests, dedicated this seat from his own funds to the Augustan cult.
EDCS-13303047.summary: This is a dedication of a seat by a freedman to the Augustan cult, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13303048.text_edition: \n @(Obitus) Vinusius Viohossii filius Cahenna Sambilii filia uxsor sic\n
EDCS-13303048.translation: The death of Vinusius, son of Viohossus, Cahenna, daughter of Sambilius, his wife.
EDCS-13303048.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of a married woman named Cahenna and her husband Vinusius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13303049.text_edition: \n Dibus Axoniebus Inua pro filia Urbicus\n
EDCS-13303049.translation: For the gods of Axonia, Urbicus [dedicated this] for his daughter.
EDCS-13303049.summary: This is a votive offering by Urbicus to the gods of Axonia for his daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13303050.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the underworld gods, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13303053.text_edition: \n Deae Tutelae Boudigae Marcus Aurelius Lunaris IIIIIIvir Augustalis coloniarum Eboraci et Lindi provinciae Britanniae inferioris aram quam voverat ab Eboraci sic avectus votum solvit libens merito Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus\n
EDCS-13303053.translation: Marcus Aurelius Lunaris, one of the six imperial priests of the colonies of Eboracum and Lindum in the province of Lower Britain, freed from his vow and gladly and deservedly fulfilled the altar which he had vowed, having been conveyed from Eboracum, with Perpetuus and Cornelianus as consuls.
EDCS-13303053.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Boudiga by Marcus Aurelius Lunaris, a high-ranking local official, commemorating the fulfillment of a vow during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13400252.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Veranus Sergia IIvir bis hanc viam restituit Cae Valerianus amicus\n
EDCS-13400252.translation: Lucius Valerius Veranus and Sergius, two-time chief magistrates, restored this road; Cae Valerianus, a friend [paid for it].
EDCS-13400252.summary: This is an inscription recording the restoration of a road by two chief magistrates, Lucius Valerius Veranus and Sergius, with a friend Cae Valerianus possibly contributing, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13900499.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis Eutyches Auggustorum nnostrorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-13900499.translation: Eutyches gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the August Deities of our Emperors.
EDCS-13900499.summary: This is a votive inscription by Eutyches to the August Deities, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14200124.translation: [To the Great Mother of the Gods] Ideae, for the health of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Felix Augustus Britannicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul twice, twice designated consul, father of the country, Caius Iulius Drutedo and Balorice performed the taur(obolium), in accordance with their vow.
EDCS-14200124.summary: This is a religious inscription recording a taurabolium sacrifice performed by Caius Iulius Drutedo and Balorice for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15200181.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI consuli II patri patriae proconsuli civitate Lemovicum leugae XXX\n
EDCS-15200181.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul twice, father of the country, proconsul, from the territory of the Lemovices, 30 leugae away.
EDCS-15200181.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating a distance of 30 leugae from the territory of the Lemovices, referencing Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-16500148.translation: Lucius Iulius Aeto made [this] for Sabinus.
EDCS-16500148.summary: This is a dedication of a monument or object by Lucius Iulius Aeto for Sabinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20500268.text_edition: \n Leontio Leontio Deidio Iovino inbolaverunt sic manus pedis quicumqui sic levavit annue culi qui i immer<g=I>o irue \n
EDCS-20500268.translation: Leontius and Leontius Deidius Iovinus dedicated it. Whoever lifts this with hand or foot, may anger of the gods be upon him.
EDCS-20500268.summary: This is a curse inscription, likely from a tomb, warning against desecration.

EDCS-20500269.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Iulio Quinto defuncto annorum XXI POS POS CVN\n
EDCS-20500269.translation: To the departed spirits and the memory of Julius Quintus, who died at the age of 21.
EDCS-20500269.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young man named Julius Quintus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200270.text_edition: \n Cesido urciu CXXI congialdi XXV Melauso urciu LVI souxtu CC scutra V Atticco trisextia LXX congialdi XXV souxtu CXXV Veriduco congialdi XIIII trisextia XXX suxtu C Cintumo souxtu CXXX\n
EDCS-24200270.translation: Cesidus: 121 urns, 25 congius jars. Melausus: 56 urns, 200 measures. Atticcus: 70 measures, 25 congius jars, 125 measures. Veriducus: 14 congius jars, 30 measures, 100 measures. Cintumus: 130 measures.
EDCS-24200270.summary: This inscription records quantities of measures of liquids, likely wine or oil, possibly related to a contract or inventory.

EDCS-24200271.text_edition: \n Lucio Iulio Luci filio Bellico hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-24200271.translation: This monument will not pass to the heir of Lucius Julius Bellicus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-24200271.summary: This is a funerary inscription that explicitly states the monument is not to be inherited, likely indicating a specific burial plot arrangement.

EDCS-28800644.text_edition: \n ea tribi talantobi us gabas Senaucos\n
EDCS-28800644.translation: To you, Talantobius, Gabas Senaucos [dedicated this].
EDCS-28800644.summary: This is a dedication to a deity named Talantobius, with the dedicant identified as Gabas Senaucos, likely of Gallic origin.

EDCS-30101155.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Buculi Nammii filii et Titullae Homuli filiae uxori posterisque eorum\n
EDCS-30101155.translation: To the departed spirits of Buculus, son of Nammius, and his wife Titulla, daughter of Homulus, and their descendants.
EDCS-30101155.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Buculus, his wife Titulla, and their descendants.

EDCS-36400085.text_edition: \n Deo Ageioni Sembus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-36400085.translation: Probus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the god Ageio Sembus.
EDCS-36400085.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to the god Ageio Sembus.

EDCS-39200752.translation: Remains of Loukillis, mother of twins. Here lie the infants whom she divided between her living husband and her grave.
EDCS-39200752.summary: This is a funerary epigram in Greek for Loukillis, lamenting the loss of her twins and her own death.

EDCS-41500060.text_edition: \n nisi V funere publico honoratus filia Unagilla \n
EDCS-41500060.translation: Honored by a public funeral, daughter Unagilla.
EDCS-41500060.summary: This inscription records that Unagilla, daughter of [name missing], was honored with a public funeral.

EDCS-44100188.text_edition: \n <F=E>ulguri deo Probus votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-44100188.translation: Probus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the god Fulgor.
EDCS-44100188.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow to the god Fulgor (Lightning).

EDCS-44100189.text_edition: \n Vivus Marcus Erucius Saturninus Marci Eruci Comici libertus sibi et Amabili uxsori sic et Laetae filiae et suis\n
EDCS-44100189.translation: While alive, Marcus Erucius Saturninus, freedman of Marcus Erucius Comicus, [dedicated this] for himself, and for his wife Amabilis, and for his daughter Laeta, and for his own [family].
EDCS-44100189.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for himself and his family members.

EDCS-47300119.translation: Sulpicia Albina, daughter of Sulpicius Tertius, who [dedicated] the taurobolium...
EDCS-47300119.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Sulpicia Albina and a ritual called taurobolium, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-47300146.translation: To the departed gods. Victorina, his wife, took care to place [this monument] and dedicated it under the axe for Valerius Felix, a citizen of Aqua Augusta, deceased, aged 40.
EDCS-47300146.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Valerius Felix, a citizen of Aqua Augusta, and was erected by his wife, Victorina.

EDCS-47300148.translation: [To the departed gods] and [in memory] of [---]dia, deceased, aged 65; her husband took care to place [this monument].
EDCS-47300148.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a woman named Dia, erected by her husband.

EDCS-47300155.text_edition: \n Bella defuncta annorum XXV Ianuarius defunctus annorum \n
EDCS-47300155.translation: Bella, deceased, aged 25; Ianuarius, deceased, aged...
EDCS-47300155.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the deaths of Bella and Ianuarius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-47300156.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph for a woman named Cinturina, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-47300157.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Clymenus as the freedman of Albinus, with an unclear abbreviation following.

EDCS-47300158.translation: For Julia [---], deceased, aged [---]; [she erected this monument].
EDCS-47300158.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for a woman named Julia, indicating that a monument was erected for her, likely by a relative.

EDCS-47500992.text_edition: \n Pompeius Recinus Apollini ex vissu sic\n
EDCS-47500992.translation: Pompeius Recinus [dedicated this] to Apollo, according to a vision.
EDCS-47500992.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made to the god Apollo by Pompeius Recinus, prompted by a divine vision.

EDCS-51900076.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly in Latin or a related language, with an unknown meaning.

EDCS-52000065.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely indicating ownership by someone named Pompeius, possibly in the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-52300950.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Domitiano Augusto Germanico consuli XIII censori perpetuo pontifici maximo patri patriae \n
EDCS-52300950.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, Consul for the 13th time, Censor for life, Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland.
EDCS-52300950.summary: This is a formal titulary inscription for Emperor Domitian, dating to his reign (81-96 CE).

EDCS-52301602.summary: This inscription likely denotes ownership or a dedication by Gaius Oppius Restitutus, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-52301617.summary: This inscription likely denotes ownership or a dedication by Gaius Oppius Restitutus, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-52700468.translation: Gartos, Tascos, Bristas willingly paid their vow: 4.5 units.
EDCS-52700468.summary: This inscription records three individuals fulfilling a vow, possibly related to a military or religious context, from the Roman Gaulish period.

EDCS-52700469.summary: This fragmentary inscription, possibly Celtic or Gallo-Roman, seems to record names and a numerical quantity, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-53801081.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or a dedication by Munatius Treptus, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-53901142.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or a dedication by Munatius Treptus, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-53901374.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or a dedication by Munatius Treptus, probably from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-55900131.translation: [---]etius to Mars willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-55900131.summary: This inscription records an individual named -etius fulfilling a vow to the god Mars, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-56100858.summary: This inscription records the name of a person, Verecundus, possibly from Aricanum, on an unspecified monument.

EDCS-56100881.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus and Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar.
EDCS-56100881.summary: This is a dedication to the Roman emperors Marcus Julius Philippus (Philip I) and his son Marcus Julius Philippus Caesar (Philip II), likely dating to their co-reign (244-247 CE).

EDCS-56100933.translation: Phoebus made (it). // Phoebus // Phoebus // fashions // [---] / [---] // Macrinus.
EDCS-56100933.summary: This inscription, possibly on a sculpture or artwork, states that Phoebus made it and includes the name Macrinus, perhaps the patron or another associated person.

EDCS-62301348.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Munatius Adiectus, likely on a tombstone or private monument.

EDCS-62500568.translation: From the pottery workshop of Quintus.
EDCS-62500568.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or origin from the pottery workshop of Quintus.

EDCS-67600105.summary: This inscription records the name of Titus Aelius Heliodorus, likely on a tombstone or private monument.

EDCS-69800683.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Pompeius (with uncertain letters), likely on a tombstone or private monument.

EDCS-70400007.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Caius Valerius (with uncertain letters), likely on a tombstone or private monument.

EDCS-71200102.summary: This inscription records the names Primus and Rasinius, possibly indicating a father and son relationship on a private monument.

EDCS-71400432.summary: This inscription records the name of Lucius Aurelius, likely on a tombstone or private monument.

EDCS-71400434.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Famius made the monument.

EDCS-71800432.translation: For Quintas, who died at 20 years of age.
EDCS-71800432.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintas, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-71800449.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Famius made the monument.

EDCS-72800111.translation: To the departed gods and to the memory of Iunidus, son of Titus Cantus Veter, of Peregrinus Optatus, veteran of the 4th Cohort of Raetians, Bocus.
EDCS-72800111.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Iunidus, a veteran of the 4th Cohort of Raetians, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72800112.translation: Reginus, son of Regulus, placed this at his own expense to the god Apollo.
EDCS-72800112.summary: This is a dedication of a statue or altar, set up at his own expense by Reginus to the god Apollo.

EDCS-73300098.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, mentioning a freedman.

EDCS-73300101.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, stating that someone made the monument.

EDCS-73300109.translation: To the Winds, [...] at his own expense [...] at his own expense.
EDCS-73300109.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly a dedication to the Winds, with repeated phrases indicating personal expense.

EDCS-73300112.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly funerary or votive, mentioning Constantius Natalis and an oracle's response.

EDCS-75600003.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely a tombstone or a building inscription, identifying Lucius Pompeius as the son of Cassianos.

EDCS-75600005.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records the name of Lucius Pompeius, possibly with an uncertain attribution.

EDCS-75600008.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Pompeius Cassianos, likely a personal name on a monument.

EDCS-76300013.translation: To the divine shades and the memory of Bolania Attica and Procula, wife of Proculus, his living wives, and for himself, Quintius Eurus, a dealer in the cloth trade, erected this while alive.
EDCS-76300013.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Quintius Eurus for his wives Bolania Attica and Procula, and himself, dating to the Roman Imperial period based on the formula D(is) M(anibus).

EDCS-78300091.translation: Lucius Paccius Pacatus, son of Andomus, his father, erected this monument for himself and his family while alive: for Lucius Remmius Andomus, his father; for Paccia Suaitio, daughter of Dami, his wife; and for Paccia Pacata, his daughter.
EDCS-78300091.summary: This funerary inscription was set up by Lucius Paccius Pacatus for his father, wife, daughter, and himself, indicating a familial burial plot.

EDCS-78300094.translation: To the divine shades and the memory of Titus Vibronius Senilis and his wife, Titus Vibronius Serenus, his son, erected this.
EDCS-78300094.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Titus Vibronius Serenus for his parents, Titus Vibronius Senilis and his wife.

EDCS-78300631.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription that likely mentions travellers, with the context and purpose unclear.

EDCS-79500120.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication to the god Seranus, with a partially preserved place name or epithet.

EDCS-79500125.summary: This is a short inscription likely dedicating something to a god, possibly named Laurusuni, with missing context.

EDCS-79600159.translation: Heuticatis if anyone has taken it away, may he perish by the penis
EDCS-79600159.summary: This is an unusual inscription, possibly a curse or a very crude warning against theft, mentioning a name (Heuticatis) and a dire consequence.

EDCS-79600170.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a person named Siveri, possibly related to Severus, with the initial 'VI' having an uncertain meaning.

EDCS-79600212.translation: Billicotas, daughter of Rebellius, dedicated this to Silvanus.
EDCS-79600212.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by a woman named Billicotas, likely from the Roman period.
